sentences.text: Mhm.
Mhm.
Yes.
The-- like I said, the government supported the Jews, even financially and morally.
They said, everything will be fine.
All the murderers would be brought to justice and stuff.
And the other government said, there'll be no room for Jews in Poland.
And those were actively [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] trying to find Jews and to hand them over to the Germans.
Yes, yes, yes.
She had it very, very hard.
She did a tremendous job.
She had to care for herself, care for us.
And she cared more for-- for us than for herself.
Because she was in very bad situations herself.
After the-- after the war, we stayed for awhile [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in a little I would say a resort.
It used to be a resort.
But it was all abandoned.
There was nobody there.
So we stayed there for awhile.
And then we went to Lodz.
Warsaw was completely destroyed.
And I couldn't find my father.
So I-- there was nothing to-- to hold us there.
So-- and there was no place to live in Warsaw anyway.
So we went to Lodz.
And why Lodz?
Pardon me?
Well, because we didn't want to sit there on the border.
Because the armies were on the-- on the river.
So we didn't know whether the Germans would now counterattack, and try to take it back or whatever.
So we want to Lodz.
And we settled there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We stayed there until '46.
In-- in 1946, [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] we went to Germany.
OK.
I want to go back before-- before we get to there.
I want to find out from you-- you had mentioned at the end of the other tape, that your sister and brother-in-law came there.
How were you able to keep in contact with them?
I mean, there was no phone.
How-- how did they know-- how did you all keep in contact with each other?
Well, everything from Mala.
She was the one who was outside.
And she could-- she could talk to people.
They-- they used to meet.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was wearing a person who was responsible of helping the people who needed help.
There were a lot of people risking their lives to help others.
Non-Jews?
Non-Jews too.
Yeah.
Because she was risking her life,
And Mrs.
Mochska.
She was risking her life.
Because if-- if the Gestapo, the SS, would find out that she kept Jews-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at least, in a best scenario, she would be in jail.
But you could be killed the same way as we would.
No.
The only time they came was at-- when they-- when they-- when they were evacuating this area.
They came in the morning.
They were taking off the logistics, the wiring and stuff.
And they brought some Ersatz coffee.
And they said they'll be back at 2:00 the same day.
And they would like to have some coffee ready.
We mingled with them.
Well, they were soldiers.
We mingled with them.
They were in the apartment, going in and out.
And we didn't bother hiding anymore.
Because we-- you know, we thought that they are so down that they-- there is a distinction between the-- Wehrmacht, which is the soldiers, the military part, and the-- and the SS.
Those were ordinary people.
You know, they are conscripted into the army.
And--
No.
Not at-- at this stage of the game.
This was a few days before.
Because a day or two afterwards, their daughter came in, Mrs.
Mochska's daughter, which I would say she was in her late 20s, early 30s.
And she said to me-- in Polish, of course-- there are so many people on the road.
So I went out, and I-- and I looked.
And I saw the Russians are coming.
[LAUGHS] And the first-- the first Russian soldier was a oriental, very short, very dirty, and very smelly.
And I grabbed him, and I kissed him.
And-- he had no belt.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I kissed him.
And he says to me, if I have vodka?
This was the first question.
I would have given him anything I had.
But I didn't have any vodka.
So you were liberated by the Russians, when they came in.
How did the Russians treat the population when they came in?
Well, afterwards, it was a different story.
They set up their artillery not far from where we lived.
And one evening, I was walking home.
And I shortcutted through a field.
And I got stopped by sentry.
And he took me to this commandant.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And he started asking me questions, whether I have seen any-- any airplanes flying?
Whether I have seen anything-- if I'm not a spy.
I said to him, I am not a spy for the Germans.
I don't know whether I'm living or I'm dead.
I'm just-- I just stepped out from a bad dream.
So he said to me, I don't believe you.
You must be a spy for the Germans.
And he kept me there until late at night.
I pleaded with him.
I said, there's a curfew.
I can't walk after 9 o'clock.
He says, you're not walking anywheres.
You're walking with us now.
This-- this was a heartwarming story.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So the next day we moved.
I didn't want to stay with them either.
So we moved to Lodz.
Only at right.
We used to go into the garden.
Once in awhile, we would pick a plum, which was that size--
--out of the tree.
Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- it was nice.
Yeah.
I-- I built a false wall and put some shelves over it.
It worked good.
It work when they came.
When the Germans came into the house, we all went in there.
But afterwards-- you see, they tried to take the population, the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] male population, at least, with them.
It was young men.
They might use them.
So some member from her family came to hide here with her.
He thought that, well, she's a widow alone.
So we stayed together.
He didn't ask any questions.
And I didn't-- I didn't volunteer anything.
So he just thought you were part of her family.
Yeah, right.
No.
Well, everybody was on the edge.
Everybody-- nobody knew what's happening, whether the Germans are moving or staying longer or whatever, or whether they're gonna attack again.
Mhm.
No, no.
Everybody has-- had to work for the Germans, of course.
My-- my father worked.
And my father and my sister were in one shop, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] repairing uniforms.
You know, they-- they took the uniforms from dead soldiers or-- I don't know.
But anyway, those were uniforms in need of prepare.
Yeah.
So they did it there.
And my father-- and my mother and my sister were in another one, in another factory.
They made rucksacks.
Those are the--
Backpacks, right.
And-- so what they did is they-- they would come and surround the whole factory, and tell everybody, all the workers, to go outside.
And then they picked a number.
Let's say if a factory employed 5,000 people, they would decide today, we'll take 1,000.
So everybody was scrambling to-- not to be among those-- those 1,000.
So the director of the factory had a lot to say.
Because the Germans don't-- didn't care whether he takes A or B or C, as long as they have their quota.
So when they-- when they took my mother and my sister, they were in need right away.
Because they had empty space.
Like I said, the-- the sideline-- the sidetrack was empty.
And the train was coming-- was due in, an empty train.
And they want to load it and keep it going.
Was the-- this was a-- Round the clock.
Constant, yeah, a constant operation.
So they took my mother and my sister then.
And my father couldn't even do a thing about it.
He tried.
He was-- he was devastated.
And a few days later, he was caught himself.
This was a story again, when they-- when they surrounded my father's-- the factory where my father worked.
And they didn't know about it.
And they ran to the fourth floor.
They were hiding there.
And the director of the factory called his name.
And he was there.
He was afraid to show his face.
But finally, a-- a SS man went up there and told him to go down.
And that's how they were saved.
Because the-- the director of the factory told them, get in this line.
Because he didn't-- he didn't go.
They demanded, I don't know what.
They demanded a quota.
And the directory of the factory put them in the group which has to stay.
That's how he was saved.
But he was-- he was picked up a few days later.
And again, he jumped out of the train-- of the moving train-- he threw the coat.
There were two little windows, in-- like in most freight cars.
So he-- he-- he took off his jacket, his coat.
And he told somebody there, throw out this jacket when I-- when I jump from this side.
Because there were soldiers on top of the train, of the wagons.
And they were shooting.
So they were shooting it.
When they saw this-- this coat flying out of the thing, they shot at the coat.
In the meantime, he jumped from the other side.
You understand what I'm saying?
Mhm.
A diversion.
That's how he saved himself.
And he came back with a group of workers who were coming back to the ghetto like I did every day.
He joined a group, and he walked in.
They-- they let everybody in.
You want to come in?
Go ahead.
So that's how.
My story was we worked at the railroad until March of 1943.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When they-- oh.
They had those selections.
When there was about 50,000 people left out a half a million--
--they assembled everybody on a big, big plaza.
And every group separately.
And they were supposed to go to a selection.
And there-- by the whim of the SS men, they would say, right or left.
This was your death destiny.
If you go to the left, you die.
If you go to the right, you live.
And out of this group-- we had over 250 people working-- they let 136.
The rest was taken away.
And they put us up in those freight cars.
We were living there.
And I've seen the writing on the wall.
Because I thought someday they'll come, surround this whole thing, and take everybody away.
So we decided to run.
And I left the group one night and didn't come back.
And of course, they took the-- a few days later.
They took everybody away.
I had this-- this place, which my sister, with Mala again, has rented, with a friend.
So I went there.
No, no.
This-- it-- it wasn't guarded.
We were on the-- on the tracks.
The train goes on the tracks.
And I just left.
I don't know.
I didn't announce that I'm gonna leave.
Yeah.
I just left and I went to this apartment.
I knew where their apartment is.
And I was let in.
I met her in-- she comes from Kalisz, which is on the German border, not far from the German/Polish border.
And her father was in the same line of business as my father.
And one day, I went to see her father on an errand from my father.
And that's how we met.
And there was a lot going on.
There was a lot of-- world-class symphony orchestra in Warsaw.
There were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] plays.
There was four or five theatres, which, by the order of the Germans, had to have only-- play only plays by Jewish writers.
You couldn't use any-- any non-Jew.
So there was a lot of culture going on.
Yeah.
When I came to the apartment, my wife was there already.
And my father-in-law, being a religious man, said, you have to get married.
Even in this apartment, in those conditions.
So we got married.
And we had a very elaborate wedding, 19 people.
And a festive meal.
She bought a couple of cupcakes.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this was everything.
But we had a very good life together.
[CRYING] [DRINKS] So you were married when you were in the apartment, with the 19 of you?
And that was your wedding party?
Yeah.
No, not really.
But I think they all survived.
No.
Well, this time when the one who came back to buy the flowers.
And then was the incident with her divorced husband.
When you closed all the windows and locked him out of the house.
Yeah, yeah.
This was the-- these were the only two cases.
Yeah, we were pretty lucky.
Yeah, yeah.
It's-- it's funny.
After this situation, where the-- the Russians came in, the next day when we started to moving out, because she thought this is proper, we were walking the street, the Main Street.
And there were tens of thousands of people going in both directions.
Because some people thought, oh, if you move north it will be better.
And some thought that if we move south it would be better.
But anyway, there was a lot, a lot of people on the road.
And I was walking the street there, too, with all the others.
And a man stops in front of me and says to me, where did your hide?
I wish I would know about you yesterday.
This put a chill down my spine.
He meant it.
He meant business.
Who was it?
He was somebody who didn't like me.
Just a-- just a ordinary man who didn't like Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So he says, if he would only found me-- only find me yesterday.
He would probably denounce me.
He would probably bring me to the Germans.
I just looked at him.
I didn't say a word.
I was just looking for my father.
I thought I'll get some information.
And the-- the house was destroyed.
People used to live-- write something on the walls, on the remaining walls.
Like, I am-- they put their name down.
And they put their address where they're gonna be.
But I didn't find anything about my father, no message, nothing.
Yeah, on the walls.
Yeah.
Well, it was-- it was a chaos, all over.
We didn't know where to go.
It's getting-- it was getting dark.
And we had to find a place to sleep.
So this again was-- we hooked up with another couple.
And they said, they have-- they know a place.
They know a-- they know an address where they can go.
There's a-- a house still intact, where you can at least have a roof over your head.
So we went there.
And we slept over the night-- stayed overnight, the night.
And the next day, we moved on.
One day.
Mhm.
Yes.
Mala came from-- lived in Lodz after-- after she got married.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they couldn't get their old apartment.
It was occupied by somebody else.
So they had to go rent another apartment.
We used to come, but we had our own apartment.
But we used to meet, almost daily.
At-- at the beginning, the government helped people displaced and people without apartments, without places to live.
Because, you see, the Germans took over all the nice apartment.
And they requested furniture and-- and stuff.
So there were a lot of empty apartments too, which they have left.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we got support from the-- from the-- HIAS.
We didn't--
Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got in touch with my uncle.
And the situation in Palestine was very bad then.
It was hunger and no housing.
So he wanted to see us very, very much.
But he advised us not to come, wait a little longer.
The situation will improve, and then we will come.
And in the meantime, we had an alternative either to go to Israel, stay in Germany, go to the United States.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After we got this rejection-- I should call it-- from my uncle, we decided to go to the United States.
I believe it was three years.
You were on the waiting list.
You were in the quarters.
And one day, they let you know that if you want to go, you have two weeks or three weeks or four weeks to prepare.
Not a very large family.
But a very close one.
[LAUGHS] My sister in Israel, and the uncle in-- in Israel.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And a couple of cousins.
That was it.
Mhm.
Yeah.
My mother had two brothers and four sisters, four or five sisters.
Four sisters.
My father died.
No.
My father?
None.
Yeah.
We were in Lodz.
Well, it didn't impress us as much.
Because we-- we knew it's coming.
We saw they are pulling back and back, further and further.
And we know it's coming.
So we weren't so shocked by it.
No.
Well, I'll tell you one story.
This will reflect the whole situation.
There were-- there was-- there's a town in Poland called Kiecle, which is a big textile center.
And people usually came back to their place of birth or where they lived before the war.
So there was a house which was owned by the Jewish community before the war.
A few hundred people who came back from the-- those-- from this region, settled in this building.
And one day, somebody-- they found a Christian boy killed, a young kid.
And somebody spread the rumor that the Jews killed him because they-- they need blood for matzoh.
The mob stormed this building.
This was a year after the war-- after the war ended.
They stormed this building.
They pulled out 72 people and killed them with rocks, with picks, with knives.
They killed 72 people.
This was 50 years ago.
In Kiecle.
So this was the attitude.
